title: Add burst configuration to Control Plane API (tenant enablement, limits, priority tier, validation, audit hooks)
preview:
Context
Dedicated Burst Capacity requires a tenant-scoped configuration surface in the Control Plane so we can:
- enable/disable burst per Dedicated tenant
- set hard caps/guardrails that admission/routing can enforce deterministically
- expose read APIs to Console + support tooling
- produce audit events for all config changes (including admin overrides)

This ticket is specifically the Control Plane API + validation + audit logging hooks. The admission controller and routing integration are tracked separately.

Scope (in)
1) Tenant model/config schema
- Add a BurstConfig block on the tenant (or dedicated capacity config) with:
  - enabled: boolean
  - max_burst_ratio: float (relative to baseline reserved capacity) OR max_burst_gpus: int (absolute cap), with clear precedence rules
  - max_burst_gpus: int (optional)
  - priority_tier: enum (e.g., standard | silver | gold) used only for fairness weighting; must not allow starvation of non-priority tenants
  - cooldown_seconds: int (minimum time after sustained burst usage before admitting additional burst beyond baseline; exact semantics owned by admission controller but validated here)
  - min_burst_deny_retry_after_seconds: int (optional; used for 429 guidance / Retry-After)
  - notes: string (optional, internal-only; for CS/Sales context)

2) Control Plane APIs
- Admin API (internal) to set burst config for a tenant
  - PATCH /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
  - GET   /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
- Customer-facing read API (used by Console) to display burst enablement + limits
  - GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config (read-only; gated to tenant principals)

API response should include:
- effective_config (after defaults + validation)
- last_updated_at
- last_updated_by (redacted appropriately for customer view)

3) Validation + defaults (server-side)
- enabled default: false
- max_burst_ratio default: 1.5 for beta tenants unless explicitly overridden (safe default; can be adjusted later without schema changes)
- max_burst_ratio allowed range: [1.0, 3.0] (beta); reject outside range
- max_burst_gpus allowed range: [0, 512] with additional guard: cannot exceed fleet safety cap policy for the tenant’s region/hardware class (enforced via policy engine rule; Control Plane validates using policy evaluation results)
- priority_tier allowed values: standard, silver, gold (future-proofed: reject unknown)
- cooldown_seconds allowed range: [0, 3600]
- Precedence: if max_burst_gpus is set, admission uses min(max_burst_gpus, floor(max_burst_ratio * baseline_gpus)); if only ratio is set, cap is ratio * baseline; if neither set, use defaults.
- Reject invalid combinations:
  - enabled=true and max_burst_ratio < 1.0
  - max_burst_gpus set but tenant has no Dedicated baseline (Dedicated-only feature)
  - priority_tier != standard unless tenant has burst enabled

4) Audit logging hooks
- Emit audit events on:
  - burst_config.enabled changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_ratio changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_gpus changed
  - burst_config.priority_tier changed
  - burst_config.cooldown_seconds changed
  - burst_config overridden via emergency / admin override path (include override reason)
- Event requirements:
  - actor (user/service), actor_type, request_id, tenant_id
  - before/after (with safe redaction for internal notes)
  - source: console | api | internal_tool

Out of scope (for this ticket)
- Per-region overrides / cross-region bursting controls (explicitly out of scope for initial release)
- Customer-writable burst config (beta/GA is Redwood-enabled only; customers can view in Console)
- Admission semantics (queue vs immediate deny) beyond what’s needed for API shape

Implementation notes
- Store in tenant config model so routing + admission can consume same source of truth via existing config distribution.
- Avoid high-cardinality audit payloads; keep diffs concise.
- All writes must go through RBAC check (Control Plane)
  - internal write: platform-admin, dedicated-ops
  - customer read: tenant-admin + dedicated-viewer
- Include propagation expectations in response headers (we’ve seen customer confusion when Console lags).
  - Add "x-config-propagation-estimate-seconds" header (best-effort) OR include in response message.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] New BurstConfig fields added to tenant schema + persisted
- [x] PATCH endpoint supports partial updates and validates ranges/combos
- [x] GET endpoints return effective config (defaults applied) and metadata (last updated)
- [x] RBAC enforced for read/write paths
- [x] Audit events emitted for all config changes and admin override
- [x] Backward compatible migration (existing tenants unaffected; enabled=false default)
- [x] Unit tests for validation and RBAC; integration test for audit event emission
- [x] Docs for internal operators (runbook snippet: how to enable for a tenant; what fields mean)

Links
- PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/dedicated-burst-capacity-prd.json
- Design spec: sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/burst-pool-architecture-and-guardrails.json
- Audit events spec: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/burst-capacity-audit-events.json
- Related tickets:
  - ENG-4810 implement admission controller service
  - ENG-4821 cooldown + fairness enforcement
  - ENG-4881 denial reason codes + support tooling

Deliverables / code pointers
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-23741-add-burst-config-schema-to-tenant-model.json
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-23866-add-audit-log-events-for-burst-config-changes.json
- GitHub PR (Console read APIs): sources/github/redwood/pr-23905-console-api-endpoints-for-burst-usage.json

Notes for rollout
- Feature-gate write path behind "burst_config_writes_enabled" (default false). Enable only for internal tools during beta.
- Tenant allowlist: do not allow enabled=true unless tenant is on the Dedicated burst beta allowlist (Control Plane check; list managed by Product Ops / Dedicated Ops).

Open questions (resolved)
- Ratio vs absolute cap: support both; admission uses the tighter cap.
- Customer editability: read-only for GA v1; revisit after we have denial reason + billing UX in place.
- Audit event retention: follow standard tenant-config change retention (same as Dedicated autoscaling config).
text:
Context
Dedicated Burst Capacity requires a tenant-scoped configuration surface in the Control Plane so we can:
- enable/disable burst per Dedicated tenant
- set hard caps/guardrails that admission/routing can enforce deterministically
- expose read APIs to Console + support tooling
- produce audit events for all config changes (including admin overrides)

This ticket is specifically the Control Plane API + validation + audit logging hooks. The admission controller and routing integration are tracked separately.

Scope (in)
1) Tenant model/config schema
- Add a BurstConfig block on the tenant (or dedicated capacity config) with:
  - enabled: boolean
  - max_burst_ratio: float (relative to baseline reserved capacity) OR max_burst_gpus: int (absolute cap), with clear precedence rules
  - max_burst_gpus: int (optional)
  - priority_tier: enum (e.g., standard | silver | gold) used only for fairness weighting; must not allow starvation of non-priority tenants
  - cooldown_seconds: int (minimum time after sustained burst usage before admitting additional burst beyond baseline; exact semantics owned by admission controller but validated here)
  - min_burst_deny_retry_after_seconds: int (optional; used for 429 guidance / Retry-After)
  - notes: string (optional, internal-only; for CS/Sales context)

2) Control Plane APIs
- Admin API (internal) to set burst config for a tenant
  - PATCH /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
  - GET   /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
- Customer-facing read API (used by Console) to display burst enablement + limits
  - GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config (read-only; gated to tenant principals)

API response should include:
- effective_config (after defaults + validation)
- last_updated_at
- last_updated_by (redacted appropriately for customer view)

3) Validation + defaults (server-side)
- enabled default: false
- max_burst_ratio default: 1.5 for beta tenants unless explicitly overridden (safe default; can be adjusted later without schema changes)
- max_burst_ratio allowed range: [1.0, 3.0] (beta); reject outside range
- max_burst_gpus allowed range: [0, 512] with additional guard: cannot exceed fleet safety cap policy for the tenant’s region/hardware class (enforced via policy engine rule; Control Plane validates using policy evaluation results)
- priority_tier allowed values: standard, silver, gold (future-proofed: reject unknown)
- cooldown_seconds allowed range: [0, 3600]
- Precedence: if max_burst_gpus is set, admission uses min(max_burst_gpus, floor(max_burst_ratio * baseline_gpus)); if only ratio is set, cap is ratio * baseline; if neither set, use defaults.
- Reject invalid combinations:
  - enabled=true and max_burst_ratio < 1.0
  - max_burst_gpus set but tenant has no Dedicated baseline (Dedicated-only feature)
  - priority_tier != standard unless tenant has burst enabled

4) Audit logging hooks
- Emit audit events on:
  - burst_config.enabled changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_ratio changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_gpus changed
  - burst_config.priority_tier changed
  - burst_config.cooldown_seconds changed
  - burst_config overridden via emergency / admin override path (include override reason)
- Event requirements:
  - actor (user/service), actor_type, request_id, tenant_id
  - before/after (with safe redaction for internal notes)
  - source: console | api | internal_tool

Out of scope (for this ticket)
- Per-region overrides / cross-region bursting controls (explicitly out of scope for initial release)
- Customer-writable burst config (beta/GA is Redwood-enabled only; customers can view in Console)
- Admission semantics (queue vs immediate deny) beyond what’s needed for API shape

Implementation notes
- Store in tenant config model so routing + admission can consume same source of truth via existing config distribution.
- Avoid high-cardinality audit payloads; keep diffs concise.
- All writes must go through RBAC check (Control Plane)
  - internal write: platform-admin, dedicated-ops
  - customer read: tenant-admin + dedicated-viewer
- Include propagation expectations in response headers (we’ve seen customer confusion when Console lags).
  - Add "x-config-propagation-estimate-seconds" header (best-effort) OR include in response message.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] New BurstConfig fields added to tenant schema + persisted
- [x] PATCH endpoint supports partial updates and validates ranges/combos
- [x] GET endpoints return effective config (defaults applied) and metadata (last updated)
- [x] RBAC enforced for read/write paths
- [x] Audit events emitted for all config changes and admin override
- [x] Backward compatible migration (existing tenants unaffected; enabled=false default)
- [x] Unit tests for validation and RBAC; integration test for audit event emission
- [x] Docs for internal operators (runbook snippet: how to enable for a tenant; what fields mean)

- PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/dedicated-burst-capacity-prd.json
- Design spec: sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/burst-pool-architecture-and-guardrails.json
- Audit events spec: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/burst-capacity-audit-events.json
- Related tickets:
  - ENG-4810 implement admission controller service
  - ENG-4821 cooldown + fairness enforcement
  - ENG-4881 denial reason codes + support tooling

Deliverables / code pointers
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-23741-add-burst-config-schema-to-tenant-model.json
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-23866-add-audit-log-events-for-burst-config-changes.json
- GitHub PR (Console read APIs): sources/github/redwood/pr-23905-console-api-endpoints-for-burst-usage.json

Notes for rollout
- Feature-gate write path behind "burst_config_writes_enabled" (default false). Enable only for internal tools during beta.
- Tenant allowlist: do not allow enabled=true unless tenant is on the Dedicated burst beta allowlist (Control Plane check; list managed by Product Ops / Dedicated Ops).

Open questions (resolved)
- Ratio vs absolute cap: support both; admission uses the tighter cap.
- Customer editability: read-only for GA v1; revisit after we have denial reason + billing UX in place.
- Audit event retention: follow standard tenant-config change retention (same as Dedicated autoscaling config).

title: Filtering engine for SIEM-ready audit exports (allow/deny by category/action + actor/resource predicates)
preview:
Goal
Implement the filtering model for SIEM-ready audit exports to support enterprise requirements (noise reduction, least-data export) while preserving delivery guarantees and avoiding performance regressions.

This ticket covers:
- Runtime evaluation of export filters (exporter-side) for each audit event.
- Validation/normalization rules shared with control plane to prevent unsafe/expensive filters.
- Safe defaults and clear semantics (allowlist + denylist, deny wins).
- Performance tests to ensure filtering overhead is negligible at expected event volumes.

References
- Confluence ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-export-filtering-model.json
- Schema v2 + category taxonomy: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-event-schema-v2-and-siem-mapping.json
- Control plane export config API ticket: ENG-5132
- Exporter scaffold/config sync ticket: ENG-5139

Non-goals (v1)
- Arbitrary regex matching or JSONPath over event payloads (too risky + too expensive + hard to validate).
- Full text search over request/response bodies (prohibited by PII policy anyway).
- Cross-event correlation filtering.

Proposed semantics (v1)
1) Filter structure
- allow:
  - categories: [string]
  - actions: [string]
  - actor_types: ["user"|"service_account"|"api_key"|"system"]
  - resource_types: [string]
- deny:
  - categories/actions/actor_types/resource_types (same shapes as allow)
- predicates (optional):
  - actor_ids: [string] (exact match only; intended for service accounts / API keys)
  - resource_ids: [string] (exact match only)
  - workspace_ids: [string] (exact match)
  - source_ip_cidrs: [cidr] (optional; only evaluated when event has source_ip)

2) Matching rules
- If allow.* is empty / unset: treat as “allow all” for that dimension.
  Example: allow.categories unset means categories do not constrain.
- If allow.* is set and non-empty: event must match at least one value in each provided allow dimension.
  Example: if allow.categories AND allow.actions are set, event must match both.
- Deny is always applied. If an event matches any deny clause, it is excluded even if it matches allow.
- Predicates behave as additional allow constraints when present (i.e., must match).
- Unknown categories/actions are treated as non-matching (conservative) when allow.* is explicitly set.
- Missing optional event fields:
  - If predicate requires field (e.g., source_ip_cidrs) and event lacks it => does not match predicate (excluded if predicate is set).

3) Safe defaults
- Default export behavior when no filter is configured: export all audit events emitted by schema v2 (subject to audit event PII policy / redaction rules; filtering is not a substitute for redaction).
- For beta UI, we will recommend customers start with allow.categories only (least error-prone) and expand as needed.

Validation / guardrails
To prevent pathological configs and keep evaluation cheap:
- Hard limits:
  - max values per list: 100
  - max total values across allow+deny+predicates: 400
  - max CIDRs: 50
- Strings:
  - exact match only (no regex, no wildcards)
  - max length 128
- CIDR validation:
  - IPv4 and IPv6 supported; parsed at config load.
- Normalization:
  - de-duplicate lists
  - lower-case category/action tokens (category taxonomy is lowercase per schema v2)
- Config load failure behavior:
  - If filter fails validation, the exporter should mark destination as “config_error” and NOT export until fixed (avoid accidentally exporting too much).

Implementation notes
- Implemented as a small, allocation-friendly matcher compiled once per (tenant, destination) config refresh.
- Representation:
  - allow/deny token lists stored as hash sets for O(1) lookups.
  - CIDRs compiled to netaddr objects and checked only if event.source_ip exists and source_ip_cidrs is non-empty.
- Evaluation order (fast-fail):
  1) deny tokens (category/action/actor_type/resource_type)
  2) allow tokens
  3) predicate exact matches (workspace_id, actor_id, resource_id)
  4) CIDR predicate
- Observability:
  - exporter increments counters per destination for filtered_out_total with labels {reason=deny|allow_miss|predicate_miss} (label cardinality constrained; no category/action labels).
  - exporter logs per-batch summary at debug (not info) to avoid noisy logs.

Performance tests
Added microbench + load test coverage:
- Bench scenario: 10k events batch, 3 allow dimensions, 1 deny dimension, 10 CIDRs.
- Target: p95 evaluation overhead <= 5ms per 10k events on baseline exporter instance type (filtering should be dwarfed by encoding + network IO).
- Added regression test in CI to fail if overhead increases by >25% vs pinned baseline.

Acceptance criteria
- Export config supports allowlist/denylist by category/action/actor_type/resource_type.
- Export config supports optional predicates for actor_id/resource_id/workspace_id and source_ip CIDRs.
- Deny overrides allow.
- Empty allow dimension does not constrain.
- Exporter behavior on invalid filter is fail-closed (no export) with surfaced status.
- Unit tests cover edge cases (missing fields, unknown categories, empty lists, duplicates).
- Bench tests exist and run in CI (or nightly) with documented thresholds.

Rollout considerations
- Behind SIEM export beta feature flag; matcher compiled only for enabled tenants.
- Requires schema v2 categories/actions to be stable enough for customers to build allowlists; PM to provide canonical category list in docs.

Follow-ups (out of scope here)
- “Preview filter” endpoint in control plane / Console that shows sample counts by category over last N minutes (requires aggregation store).
- Support for “starts_with” on resource_id for hierarchical resource naming (needs careful validation).
- Tenant-level default denylist for known noisy system actions (needs Security review to avoid hiding security-relevant events).

title: Control plane: annotate sensitive endpoints with RBAC v2 permissions + add coverage gate
preview:
## Context
RBAC v2 requires deny-by-default authorization enforcement across Gateway + Control Plane services. Gateway middleware (ENG-18277) will block requests unless the route is mapped to a permission and the request is authorized.

We are currently missing consistent permission annotations on several Control Plane endpoints (org/user/role management, routing policy updates, model pinning/rollout config, audit export controls, private deployment admin ops). This creates two risks:
1) accidental implicit allow (or inconsistent allow) when checks are skipped
2) drift over time: new endpoints ship without annotations/permission mapping

This ticket covers Control Plane endpoint annotation + a test/CI gate to ensure no unannotated *sensitive* endpoints ship.

## Goals
- Every security-relevant Control Plane endpoint has an explicit RBAC v2 permission annotation.
- Enforcement path is exercised in tests (at least at interceptor/router layer).
- CI gate prevents adding new sensitive endpoints without annotations.
- Clear, reviewed exemptions for endpoints that must be unauthenticated/authorization-free (e.g., health checks) with documented rationale.

## Non-goals
- Defining the permission catalog itself (see permission inventory + registry work).
- Implementing the Gateway deny-by-default middleware (separate ticket).
- Building full ABAC / policy language.

## Scope (services/endpoints)
Primary services:
- control-plane-api (REST) + control-plane-gateway (internal)
- org-service: org/project/env CRUD, membership
- identity-service: users, groups, SSO metadata read endpoints
- rbac-service: roles, role bindings, templates
- routing-policy-service: policy CRUD, rollouts, fallback settings
- deployment-service: dedicated/private deployment settings that affect isolation/capacity

Endpoint classes that must be annotated (examples, not exhaustive):
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/api-keys: create/rotate/revoke keys
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/roles + PUT/DELETE role bindings
- PUT /orgs/{orgId}/routing-policies/{id}: edit routing policies
- PUT /orgs/{orgId}/model-pinning: change pinned model/version
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/audit-exports: start export job / configure exporter destinations
- PUT /private-deployments/{clusterId}/settings: modify private install/runtime settings

Explicit exempt endpoints (must be documented + asserted):
- GET /healthz, GET /readyz
- GET /version
- internal metrics scrape endpoints (if any)

## Implementation plan
### 1) Annotation mechanism (standard)
- Use the standard permission annotation supported by the Control Plane router/interceptor.
  - HTTP handlers: `@RequiresPermission("<permission>")` (or equivalent route metadata)
  - gRPC handlers: `RequiresPermission("<permission>")` in interceptor metadata
- All permission strings must be sourced from the permission registry (no raw string literals) to avoid typos and enable catalog audits.

### 2) Route coverage gate
Add a unit test that fails when:
- any endpoint in the "sensitive" namespace lacks a permission annotation
- any endpoint has an annotation that is not present in the registry
- any endpoint is exempt but not in the explicit allowlist with rationale

How we define "sensitive" for this gate:
- endpoints whose route group is tagged as `authz_required=true` (preferred)
- OR endpoints under specific route groups (e.g., /orgs, /projects, /environments, /routing-policies, /roles, /audit-exports, /private-deployments)

The initial implementation uses a conservative allowlist for exemptions while we iterate. The allowlist must live in-repo with a short comment per route.

### 3) Minimal enforcement test
- Add a focused test ensuring that the router/interceptor actually checks `RequiresPermission` metadata (e.g., returns 403/permission_denied when no permission grant is present).
- This is NOT a full policy evaluation integration test; it just asserts the enforcement path triggers.

### 4) Backwards compatibility / rollout hooks
- Annotation should work under dual-read mode (RBAC v1 + v2). Under the feature flag:
  - if RBAC v2 is enabled: permission annotations drive enforcement
  - if disabled: existing v1 checks remain (where they exist)
- Make sure denial errors follow the standardized error semantics (permission denied code + reason code).

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] 100% of in-scope Control Plane endpoints tagged as sensitive have a permission annotation.
- [x] All permission references are from the permission registry (no unvalidated free-form strings).
- [x] CI fails on new sensitive endpoints without annotations.
- [x] Exempt endpoints are explicitly listed with rationale and covered by tests.
- [x] At least one enforcement-path unit test asserts permission metadata is required and produces the correct error code.
- [x] Security Eng sign-off on exemption list + coverage gate behavior.

## Test plan
- Unit test: enumerate registered HTTP routes + gRPC methods and validate:
  - has `RequiresPermission`
  - permission exists in registry
  - if exempt, route is present in allowlist
- Unit test: enforcement path returns permission denied when principal lacks required permission.
- Optional follow-up (separate): expand e2e integration tests for critical flows (role changes, routing policy edits).

## Rollout notes
- Land annotations + tests behind existing feature flags; annotations can ship even before full v2 enforcement is enabled.
- Monitor deny rate once v2 is enabled (separate rollout runbook). If deny spikes occur, likely causes:
  - incorrect permission mapping (fix in role template)
  - missing scope propagation in request context

## Links
- Confluence: authorization check patterns (gateway vs service): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/authorization-check-patterns.json
- GitHub PR (endpoint annotations): sources/github/redwood/pr-28188-annotate-control-plane-endpoints-with-permissions.json
- Related: permission registry PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-28134-add-permission-enum-and-registry.json
- Related: Gateway deny-by-default middleware: sources/github/redwood/pr-28159-gateway-authz-middleware-deny-by-default.json

## Notes / open questions
- Do we treat read-only endpoints returning potentially sensitive metadata (e.g., SSO config, audit log list endpoints) as sensitive for the gate? Current approach: yes, unless explicitly exempted.
- Some internal-only endpoints are used by Console backend. Those should still be annotated; internal network is not a security boundary for enterprise/SOC2 expectations.

title: Create Private Grafana dashboard: Control Plane Health
preview:
Build the v1 Grafana dashboard for Redwood Private control plane health. This is part of the “Private observability minimum pack” and must ship as a supported, turnkey dashboard (bundled + versioned) via observability-pack and wired into redwood-helm-charts / private installer.

Primary users:
- Customer platform/SRE teams operating Redwood Private (VPC and on-prem)
- Redwood Support/SRE during escalations (scrape failures, gateway errors, auth/RBAC issues)

Scope (v1 dashboard):
- Control plane services and their dependencies:
  - API Gateway / ingress layer (requesting auth, routing to control plane + data plane)
  - Config service / policy engine (policy evaluation, config distribution)
  - Auth/RBAC integration points (OIDC/JWKS fetch, LDAP/SAML bridge if applicable, token verification failures)
  - Installer/upgrade controller (reconcilers, upgrade steps, failures)
  - Kubernetes health signals for the above: deployments, pods, restarts, OOMKilled, readiness/liveness
- Intended to be safe for Private clusters:
  - No high-cardinality label usage in queries (avoid raw tenant_id / request_id / full route)
  - Use recording rules where available (see ENG-19144) and standardized names/labels (ENG-19143)

Non-goals (v1):
- Customer-specific dashboards or multi-cluster federation views
- Deep per-tenant or per-route breakdowns (cardinality constraints)
- Full tracing views (we may link out if OTEL is enabled, but not required)

Dashboard requirements:
1) “At a glance” control plane status row (top of dashboard)
- Uptime / availability for API gateway and key control plane services (use up == 1 and/or service-specific health metrics)
- Current error rate (5xx, auth errors) and request volume
- p50/p95 latency for gateway and control plane endpoints
- Active alerts annotation panel (link to alert rules relevant to this dashboard)

2) API Gateway panel group
- Requests (RPS) by broad outcome class:
  - 2xx/3xx, 4xx (authn/authz vs other), 5xx
- Latency heatmap or time series for p50/p95/p99
- Upstream target health summary (control plane upstreams)
- 401/403 breakdown (authn vs authz) using bounded labels only (e.g., error_type in {invalid_token, expired_token, rbac_denied, jwks_unreachable})
- Retry/timeout counters (to identify upstream slowness)

3) Config / Policy services panel group
- Policy evaluation rate + evaluation latency (p95)
- Policy evaluation failures (by error_type)
- Config propagation / reconcile queue depth (if applicable)
- “Last successful config sync” age (gauge) + failure count
- CPU/mem saturation for these deployments + restart rate

4) Auth/RBAC integrations panel group
- JWKS/OIDC fetch success rate + last fetch age
- Token verification errors (bounded enum)
- RBAC decision errors and denials (denials should be separable from errors)
- External identity provider reachability (if metric exists) or HTTP client error counters

5) Installer/Upgrade controller panel group
- Reconcile loop health: reconcile duration p95, reconcile errors rate
- Current upgrade step (if exposed as a gauge/label with bounded values) OR show last completed step timestamp
- CrashLoopBackOff / frequent restarts

6) Kubernetes / Infra health for control plane (support-first)
- Pod restarts (rate) by deployment
- OOMKilled events count (if exported) or container restarts correlated with memory saturation
- Pending pods / scheduling failures (if kube-state-metrics is present in supported bundle)
- Node pressure signals (optional, if included in minimum pack; keep as links or minimal panels)

Implementation notes (Grafana conventions for the minimum pack):
- Datasource: Prometheus (named consistently in Helm values; avoid hardcoding datasource UID if possible)
- Time range default: last 6h
- Variables:
  - cluster (if applicable) / namespace (default to redwood-private namespace)
  - service (bounded list from known control plane services)
  - instance/pod (optional; only used in drill-down panels)
- Drill-down links:
  - Link from service panel -> “Kubernetes / pods” view filtered to that service
  - Link to relevant runbook sections (Confluence) embedded in panel description for Support
- Annotations:
  - Show alert firing annotations for control-plane-related alerts (latency burn, scrape failures, crashloops)

Metric assumptions / dependencies:
- This ticket depends on metric naming/label stabilization work (ENG-19143) so we can write stable queries.
- Prefer recording rules introduced in ENG-19144 for golden signals and standardized service-level metrics.
- Where metrics don’t exist yet (notably auth/RBAC integration metrics), this dashboard should:
  - Use whatever standardized HTTP client/server metrics exist today, and
  - Track missing metrics as follow-ups (new tickets) instead of inventing brittle/expensive queries.

Packaging requirements:
- Dashboard JSON must be committed to observability-pack (versioned bundle)
- Must be referenced in redwood-helm-charts values under the Private minimum pack toggle
- Must render in air-gapped/offline install where Grafana has no internet access (no external plugins required)

Links / references:
- Standards: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/metrics-and-alerting-standards-for-private.json
- Dashboard catalog (v1 set): sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/private-minimum-pack-dashboard-catalog.json
- SLO guidance: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/private-install-slo-guidance.json
- Wiring into Helm: ENG-19152 / sources/github/redwood-helm-charts/pr-18405-wire-observability-pack-into-private-values.json
- Dashboard bundle PR (target): sources/github/observability-pack/pr-18341-add-private-minimum-pack-dashboard-bundle.json

Open questions:
- Final control plane service list in Private v1 (confirm names/labels used for service dimension)
- Whether we can safely expose “current upgrade step” as a metric without high cardinality (bounded step names)
- If customers commonly run their own ingress (vs shipping our gateway), we may need an alternate panel set keyed off gateway metrics being absent (show ‘Not available’ + hint)

Follow-ups (create if needed):
- Add missing auth integration metrics (JWKS fetch age, token validation errors) if not present
- Add kube-state-metrics panels only if kube-state-metrics is included in supported minimum pack footprint

title: KV soft-tiering: TTL+LRU hybrid with prefix-aware safety and serializability sketch
preview: Design and implement a 'soft-tier' eviction policy that combines TTL windows and LRU scoring with explicit prefix-awareness and a set of lightweight correctness guards. Goal: reduce pathological eviction cascades during continuous batching and prefetch while preserving strong observable guarantees (serializability of cache effects w.r.t. requests) and keeping tail-latency/regressions within performance SLOs.

Goal: reduce pathological eviction cascades during continuous batching and prefetch while preserving strong observable guarantees (serializability of cache effects w.r.t. requests) and keeping tail-latency/regressions within performance SLOs.

title: Hosted API cascade insulation and rollback safepoints for customer escalations
preview: Summary: Several high-severity customer escalations in the past quarter revealed a repeating pattern: tenant burst -> edge/backplane retries -> downstream queueing -> KV cache eviction -> amplified 5xx/timeouts that impact non-offending tenants. This ticket implements a multi-layered mitigation: (1) escalation gates (a short-lived, per-tenant fast-fail / priority path for customers with open escalations), (2) differential retry budgets and jittered backoffs at the edge, and (3) observable rollback safepoints and rollout gates so we can rapidly revert or restrict new routing logic without taking the entire fleet offline. The goal is to stop blast-radius amplification while preserving SLOs for paid/enterprise tenants and maintain graceful degradation for lower tiers.

title: Spectral Tint Blend and Tokenized Interaction Scale for Charts & Controls
preview: Problem: Chart palettes and component tints currently degrade when interpolated across light/dark surfaces; border and micro-shadow tokens are inconsistent for compact controls which causes perceived jitter in dense tables. Goal: introduce a spectral-aware tint blending algorithm and a small set of new semantic tokens (Tint.* and MicroRadius.*) that produce perceptually uniform steps across color ramps and responsive radii for compact components. Deliverables: token spec (JSON + migration guide), updated Figma components, component PRs for Button/Table/Chart tokens, cross-theme QA matrix, and a lightweight migration script for consumers. Non-goal: changing visual language or color semantics for product teams — we aim to preserve existing brand anchors while improving interpolation behavior and small-radius consistency.

title: TTL enforcement + purge job for request/response logs store
preview:
Goal
Implement TTL enforcement (scheduled purge) for the request/response logs data store, driven by the retention policy evaluation result for each tenant. Must be idempotent, observable, and safe under peak load.

Context
This is part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation (“Retention controls + compliance reporting pack”). Request/response logs are one of the primary data classes called out in security reviews; we need predictable deletion semantics and evidence (metrics + exports) that retention is being applied.

In-scope
- Add TTL/purge enforcement for request/response logs store ("request_logs" service DB/table/collection).
- Wire purge behavior to evaluated retention policy (per tenant, per region where applicable).
- Support safe backfills (existing rows) and ongoing incremental purging.
- Emit verification metrics and hook into standard dashboards/alerts.

Non-goals
- Per-field redaction or selective deletion (handled separately).
- Changing upstream log capture behavior (this ticket assumes logs are already being written when enabled).

Requirements / acceptance criteria
1) Correctness
- For tenants with request logging enabled: delete records older than configured retention TTL (as evaluated by control plane) with no tenant cross-contamination.
- For tenants with request logging disabled: no purge work should be performed for the request_logs store (or it should be a no-op); policy evaluation should return a disabled state and purge job should skip.
- Purge semantics: hard-delete (not tombstone) unless store-specific constraints require soft-delete; if soft-delete is used, include a follow-up task to guarantee physical deletion within a bounded window.
- Idempotency: re-running the job for the same time window does not error and does not create inconsistent state.

2) Performance and safety constraints
- Purge job must not cause sustained write amplification or lock contention on the request_logs store.
- Must operate with bounded work per run:
  - limit rows per batch
  - limit time per run
  - pacing between batches to avoid saturating DB
- Prefer index-friendly predicates (created_at < cutoff) and avoid full scans.
- Provide configuration knobs (via service config) for max rows/run, max runtime/run, and concurrency.
- Must degrade gracefully when lag/backlog exists (job keeps making progress; surfaces backlog; does not crash-loop).

3) Observability and verification
Emit (at minimum) per-store metrics:
- retention_ttl_lag_seconds{store="request_logs", tenant_tier, region} (distribution or gauge; avoid high-cardinality tenant labels)
- retention_purge_rows_deleted_total{store="request_logs", result="success|error"}
- retention_purge_failures_total{store="request_logs", error_class}
- retention_purge_backlog_seconds{store="request_logs"} (if backlog is computed; otherwise document how ttl_lag is interpreted)

Dashboards/alerts:
- Add panels for request_logs TTL lag and purge failure rate.
- Alerting guidance: page on sustained purge failures; ticket on increasing TTL lag over threshold.

4) Operational readiness
- Runbook updated/linked with specific request_logs details (where data lives, query predicate, batching knobs, how to pause/resume, how to validate).
- On-call friendly: include “DB pressure” mitigation steps (throttle, reduce batch size, disable concurrency, temporarily pause job).

Implementation notes / proposed approach
- Use the generic purge job framework (ENG baseline) with a request_logs-specific “deleter” implementation.
- Determine cutoff = now - evaluated_ttl for each tenant (or tenant group) and delete in chunks.
- Group work to avoid per-tenant loops where possible:
  - If store schema supports tenant_id + created_at index, perform deletes by tenant in bounded loops but ensure we don’t open unbounded transactions.
  - If we need to avoid tenant-cardinality in job scheduling, batch tenants into “shards” based on hash(tenant_id) and process shard ranges.
- Enforce upper bound: do not attempt to delete more than N rows/run; stop and report partial progress.

Testing plan
- Unit tests: cutoff calculation, idempotency, skip behavior when disabled.
- Integration tests in staging:
  - Create synthetic logs across time windows; verify deletion after job runs.
  - Induce transient DB errors; verify retry behavior and failure metrics.
  - Verify job respects throttles and does not exceed configured runtime.
- Load/perf checks:
  - Run job against a dataset approximating production cardinality and confirm no sustained DB CPU spike beyond agreed guardrails.

Rollout plan
- Stage 1: internal tenants only (feature flag / allowlist), 48h monitoring.
- Stage 2: design partners (Dedicated + Private where applicable), 1 week monitoring.
- Stage 3: broad enablement with SRE sign-off and dashboards live.

Open questions
- Confirm request_logs store partitioning strategy (if partitioned by day/week, deletion can drop partitions for faster performance).
- Confirm whether we should compute and store “oldest_remaining_timestamp” for lag computation vs deriving from table scans (preferred: derive from job progress + cutoff, avoid expensive queries).

Owner notes
Primary owner: Telemetry (Logan Wright). Secondary: SRE guardrails (Sean Gallagher / Allison Grant). Control plane policy evaluation owner for wiring: Jordan Lee.

title: Implement routing policy: map Dedicated tier → hardware pool/shape selection
preview:
We need a deterministic and auditable mapping from customer Dedicated tier (Standard/Plus/Premium) to eligible hardware pools/shapes in the router/orchestration layer.

This is required to:
- enforce tier-specific throughput floors by ensuring requests land on the right capacity class
- preserve Dedicated isolation semantics while still allowing defined burst/failover behaviors
- make capacity accounting and Sales quoting consistent (tier → shape families per region)

Scope:

title: Quant artifact anchoring and rollback ledger for reproducible calibration
preview:
Problem: We currently produce FP8/INT8 quant artifacts and calibration snapshots without a single authoritative, signed record that binds the artifact bytes to the calibration inputs, quantization config, toolchain, and model binary. That makes retrospective verification, reproducible rollbacks, and automated safety checks brittle.

Goal: Implement an artifact anchoring ledger and CLI verification flow that: (1) computes deterministic content fingerprints for model binaries, calibration datasets, quant profiles and build metadata; (2) publishes a signed, append-only ledger record per quant release containing the fingerprints, human-readable manifest, and storage pointers; (3) provides a verification tool to replay/validate calibration results and detect bit-rot or untrusted modifications; (4) surface an automated rollback playbook when verification or accuracy validation fails.

Success criteria include deterministic hashing of outputs, signed manifests using KMS-backed keys, nightly automated verification against goldensets, and an operator playbook for rollback that is safe for Dedicated/Private tenants.

title: Config Safety Net: causal-trace rollback, transient patching, and economic rebalancer
preview: Deliver an automated safety net for config changes that (1) identifies the causal config change for regressions using causal tracing, (2) applies a transient patch rollback that isolates the faulty change without impacting unrelated routes, and (3) triggers an "economic rebalancer" that temporarily adjusts batching/caching knobs to limit cost exposure while preserving latency SLOs.

title: Payload sparsifier & run-length microbatcher with kernel-cost evaluator for prefill/decode trimming
preview:
Problem: At 99th-percentile load we see wasted token compute from padding-heavy micro-batches where a small fraction of long sequences force larger kernel selection and reduce packing density. This increases per-token cost and occasionally pushes decode latency over our SLOs when kernel switching occurs mid-decode.

Goal: Implement a microbatch shaping component (Payload Sparsifier + Run-Length Batcher) that dynamically trims and groups prefills/decodes by runtime-observed payload patterns and a kernel-cost evaluator. The component should: (1) reduce padded tokens by opportunistic run-length trimming and prefix concatenation where semantics allow, (2) choose kernel variants based on a cost model (latency vs compute) and predicted sequence-length distribution, (3) schedule prefill and decode phases to avoid expensive kernel swaps during time-critical decode windows, and (4) expose safe fallbacks for correctness and auditability.

Scope: Prototype in the inference runtime as an opt-in scheduling pass behind a feature flag. Focus on transformer models with sparse attention and KV cache prefill. Do not change model weights or tokenizer. Provide metrics to evaluate cost impact (tokens saved, kernel-switch frequency, p99 latency delta).

Non-goals: Full PL/SQL rollout automation, cross-region routing, and new quantization algorithms. Not targeting batched embeddings endpoints in this ticket (follow-up).

title: SAML SSO: IdP-specific integration playbook for RBAC v2, audit logging, and admin controls
preview: Consolidate IdP-specific compatibility notes, verification steps, and mitigation patterns for Okta, Azure AD (Entra), Google Workspace, and Ping Identity with respect to RBAC v2, enhanced audit logging, and admin control surfaces. This ticket produces a playbook for engineering, an integration checklist for solutions engineering, and a test-suite matrix for QA. The goal is to reduce onboarding friction for enterprise customers and close known swallow/attribute-mapping gaps that cause admin/control regressions and audit inconsistencies.

title: Rotation scoping mismatch causing privilege escalation between orgs during token refresh
preview: Multiple customers reported unexpected cross-organization access following API key rotations and token refreshes in Dedicated and Private deployments. Investigation shows that under a rare combination of rolling rotations and scaled routing, the token refresh endpoint returned a session token scoped incorrectly (missing tenant binding), allowing a refreshed token to be used against a different org's resources. Hosted deployments are largely unaffected due to a separate auth proxy that enforces org headers; the issue appears in our self-hosted auth-service path used by Dedicated/Private customers. This ticket tracks the immediate remediation, root cause fix, test coverage, rollout, and customer communications.

title: Mini-carousel quickstart: compact hosted-API screenshots + code-toggle
preview:
Goal: produce a compact, mobile-first set of Hosted API getting-started screenshots for the Quickstart micro-tour. Asset set should demonstrate: model selector microflow, inline request builder (prompt presets + param sliders), code-snippet toggle (Python/Node/cURL), token-cost preview overlay, and accessibility alt-text examples. Deliverables will be a carousel of 6 hero screens (desktop + mobile variants), export-ready PNGs and an associated code snippet text bundle for docs. Focus on clarity for first-time engineers and product-led sign-up flow.

Key design constraints: compact layout that fits 640x880 (mobile) and 1280x720 (web) hero slots; code snippet lines limited to 8 for thumbnails; visible cost-estimate pill (tokens/$) in top-right; accessible color contrast >= 4.5:1; keyboard focus states shown for model selector.

Acceptance criteria:
- 6 unique frames covering: (1) Welcome + model picker open, (2) Model details overlay with capability badges, (3) Request builder showing prompt presets and system message toggle, (4) Param sliders (max_tokens, temperature, top_p) + token-cost preview, (5) Code toggle showing Python snippet, and (6) Code toggle showing Node + cURL compact variants.
- Each frame: desktop and mobile exports, named with convention: hosted_quick_XXX_{desktop|mobile}.png.
- Designer notes and alt-text for each image included in a single text file for docs teams.
- PR created with assets + snippet bundle; screenshots validated in Staging docs site.

Non-goals: full-page console walkthroughs, deep dive into Dedicated or Private flows.
